text: Today we're going to have a little go at making these chest of drawers look really nice and new.
It's an oak chest of drawers, I love it, it was expensive at the time but it's looking a bit naff
and dead so I want to sand it down, make it look pristine and new and then paint it. Everything we
need for today is on the table already so it's not much, it's very very easy and the main thing
is our sander. Now we have this really light easy sander and lithium-ion batteries as well which
makes everything so much easier, they're lighter and it just makes the job perfect. The golden
rule of sanding is that it's a lot like going to the hairdressers and having your hair cut,
you can take it off but you can't put it back. Safest thing is to start with a medium grade
sandpaper because then you'll find out pretty quickly if you need something stronger or just
a finer sandpaper. This is dead easy because it's like
velcro, it's a bit of a pain in the ass to get it out of the way. So I'm going to start with a
velcro, you can see the material there, so simple just to put it on and whip it off.
Well it's only a few steps really, I'm just going to start off by stripping it with the medium
grade, I'm then going to finish it off nicely with a finer grade, wipe it all down and it's
ready to paint. Other stuff you need to remember really is to keep safe so make sure you get
yourself a pair of goggles and a mask as well and you're ready to go.
So now we're going to paint it. I wanted to do a slightly different look, I wanted to paint it and then wipe it down.
We're going to sponge it down straight after we paint it to give it that sort of limed oak look, a very shabby chic look.
So we're not going to use a paintbrush, we're going to use a spray gun. The technique with this is very, very simple.
There is a little bit of setup as well. You've got the pot here which you put your paint in. You can choose whether you want to spray vertically or horizontally,
depending on what you're painting and you've got the air pressure here so you can do a very, very strong fast spray or you can bring it and calm it down a little bit as well.
The technique with the spray gun is easy, just keep nice even motions and don't get too close.
That was literally as easy as it looked and it's done really great coverage for us as well.
Now I'm going to get straight onto the sponging before it dries. Just a wet sponge, simple, not soaking wet. You want to make sure you wring it out.
Then you're going to go in and start wiping it all off thoroughly and quite literally start wiping it all off again.
You'll feel like you're undoing all your hard work but it's worth it and it'll look great.
So there you have it, I've taken most of the paint off leaving quite literally what has seeped into the grain there to give it that really old shabby chic look.
But again completely different to how we started off.
So a really simple job, didn't take me very long. The sander made pretty quick short work of it. The spray gun again made life a lot, lot easier for me.
When you look at your old oak chest or your set of drawers or something that's sat in the kitchen for years and you think, oh I can't be bothered to get rid of it, it'll take so long to fix it.
Just think about it, literally with the right tools that didn't take me not even an hour and a half.
It won't take you long to really revamp something and make it look fantastic.
